chunk_summary: This section outlines admission thresholds for nutrition failure in chronic cardiorespiratory disease, focusing on clinical stability, growth trajectory, and feeding safety to determine inpatient versus outpatient management.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for inpatient admission based on clinical stability, growth trajectory, and feeding safety.
Identify numeric thresholds for weight-for-length z-score decline and weight loss in pediatric patients.
Recognize feeding safety concerns, including aspiration risks and recurrent pneumonias.
Apply WHO weight-velocity standards and pediatric malnutrition indicators for early detection of nutrition failure.
key_facts: Admission is indicated for respiratory or hemodynamic instability during feeding, such as tachypnea, cyanosis, or desaturation.
Weight-for-length/height z-score decline by ≥1 (moderate concern) or ≥2 (high concern) signals admission need.
Weight loss thresholds: ≥5% in children 2–20 years (moderate concern) or ≥10% (high concern).
Persistent intake <75% of estimated needs or inability to complete feeding plans for >3–5 days warrants admission.
WHO weight-velocity standards should be used for infants under 24 months to detect early growth failure.
reference_quote: Use WHO weight-velocity standards to detect 'velocity failure' early rather than waiting for severe underweight. (Reference 2)
document_key_topics: Clinical stability and feeding safety in chronic cardiorespiratory disease
Growth trajectory monitoring and admission thresholds
Pediatric malnutrition indicators and weight-velocity standards
